findings.description: Session tokens are generated with random.choice() (Mersenne Twister PRNG), which is not cryptographically secure; observing enough emitted tokens lets an attacker reconstruct the MT19937 state and predict future session tokens, enabling session hijacking / auth bypass (CWE-330/338, OWASP A07/A02) — must use the secrets module / secrets.token_urlsafe.
Importing and using the `random` module for security-sensitive token material is the root cause of the predictable-token vulnerability; the import should be `secrets`.

touch() renews expiry via a raw dict lookup without calling _purge_expired() or checking expires_at <= now, so a logically-expired-but-unpurged session can be revived and kept alive indefinitely, defeating the TTL/idle-timeout control (OWASP A07 insufficient session expiration).

create() imposes no cap on total or per-user session count and _purge_expired() only removes expired entries, so an attacker can loop create() to allocate unbounded Session objects until TTL elapses, exhausting memory (resource-exhaustion DoS).
user_sessions() does not purge expired sessions before returning, so it can hand callers logically-expired sessions that may be treated as valid, undermining 'log out everywhere' / enumeration logic.

Token lookup relies on dict key comparison rather than a constant-time compare; a timing side-channel on token validation is largely theoretical for in-memory dict lookups but worth noting for defense-in-depth.

get() purges before returning so it correctly rejects expired tokens, but the inconsistent purge-on-some-methods-only design (get/active_count purge; touch/user_sessions do not) is an error-prone pattern that invites expiry-bypass regressions.
